title: The new age of IDEs? A review of Cursor
author: Bap
text:
The new age of coding and IDEs? A review of Cursor
The world of IDEs has seen much debate on which integrated development environment stands out as the best.
Cursor recently gained popularity in the developer community for its AI-native features. This made it an exciting IDE to experiment with.
After using it extensively for 2–3 weeks, I found the product feature-rich and the user experience intuitive and delightful.
In this article, we will thus deep dive into:
1. What added value does it bring to devs?
2. What features stand out? An Example Workflow
3. How much does it cost?
4. What are its limitations?
5. Personal experience & closing thoughts.
1. What added value does it bring to devs?
Cursor is an AI code editor forked from VS Code.
Apart from its slick and minimalist design, Cursor leverages the leading LLMs and proprietary models to speed up the process of coding, editing, and deploying code.
The primary value for developers is significantly faster development, allowing them to reclaim a non-trivial amount of time.
Cursor’s core strength lies in leveraging AI to enhance:
- code production
- refactoring
- debugging
- testing
- automating tasks
2. What features stand out? An Example workflow
To illustrate Cursor’s main features, let’s imagine you are building a simple app that allows you and your friends to share your favourite places.
Let’s leverage this use case to shed light on 3 powerful avenues where Cursor is improving the developer experience:
1. Tab completion
When first conceptualising your project, you list the main steps in building your application and start creating your first files.
This includes building a login page, a navbar, a component to share places, etc.
As you write, Cursor will automatically detect whether you are adding your comment to list out a plan, define a code snippet or write a coding instruction.
Cursor will provide suggestions, and executes on this with a surprisingly low latency and high relevancy.
The suggestions provided are accurate and capable of generating multi-line code, even for complex logic.
As you continue to develop the foundational layers of your application, Cursor will also identify areas where it can smart rewrite or even create multi-line edits.
2. Chat with AI
As with any development workflow, you are writing your authentication file and encounter a bug.
Cursor provides the opportunity to open up a window to directly chat with AI models to solve your issue. One neat feature is you can filter for whichever leading LLM models you prefer.
However, and this is where Cursor made a significant delta, you can debug by referencing your entire code base.
This means that Cursor can deep dive into `@function` or analyse the entire project by referencing `@codebase`.
Cursor’s proprietary codebase indexing is what enables this. The process involves computing embeddings for each file in your codebase, which enables to improve the accuracy of codebase-related answers.
Once the solution has been given, your code can be instantly amended by clicking on Apply. You can also ask Cursor to perform retrievals (RAG) off images, documentation and web links.
3. Agent
In your development flow for sharing places with your friends, you decided to custom-build your own validator functions.
You would like to write a function to force a user to add special characters to their password.
With Cursor’s agent, you can directly ask it to execute this task end-to-end.
Devs can leverage the agent to find context in your codebase, write and run commands or detect lint errors automatically. Each task will require your approval, and once given, the agent will execute it.
3. How much does it cost?
Cursor is a paid product but its IDE is available for free for 2 weeks.
This provides a decent experimentation window and a good opportunity to play with its main features.
Following this, the basic Pro plan starts at $20 per month with unlimited Completions, 500 fast premium requests per month, and more.
Cursor also provides a business plan for $40/month with enterprise-level enforcement on privacy and security.
4. Drawbacks
Cursor’s product is helping push the developer experience to new heights.
However, it does highlight 2 main drawbacks common to AI-native development*.
1. Security and privacy: Overreliance on AI for auto-completion and suggestion can be dangerous from a security and privacy standpoint. Developers should note that LLM model suggestions are not always accurate or secure. Providing your codebase’s access to LLMs can also be a vulnerability when dealing with organisations’ sensitive data.
2. Limits to complex projects: Though Cursor is known to leverage AI across your entire codebase, it inherently relies on models that are imperfect. This means that as projects scale, the model starts struggling with providing accurate outputs.
*In Tessl’s podcast, founder Guy Podjarny sheds light on the concept of AI-native development being difficult to define. Indeed, we are in the process of discovering what it is and what it should look like. As of now, we can see it as any tool that leverage AI to improve any part of the software development lifecycle.
5. Personal experience & closing thoughts
I always enjoyed playing with IDEs.
I remember my first IDE being PyCharm, and completely fell in love with it! Later on, my manager inspired me to try out Atom and I resonated with its minimalist style. VS code was then my preferred IDE as its plugin ecosystem and git integration made the development process smooth and enjoyable.
As I heard rumours around me of Cursor being a “game-changer”, I thought it would be exciting to experiment with it.
What particularly struck me is the seamless developer experience. Historically, if we leveraged the help of LLMs when developing, we were constrained to leave our IDE and jump to a browser tab.
We would also find frustration in the lack of recall from some of the LLMs inside our thread. Cursor removed this barrier and enabled developers to provide both a birds-eye and granular view of your codespace without ever leaving your IDE.
After a two-week trial, I believe Cursor is worth continuing to use. The main reasons lay in the fact that I saved a non-trivial amount of time and felt the price point to be reasonable enough to commit to a paid subscription (I seldom pay money for developer tools).
It goes without saying that this decision is subjective and I believe other alternatives are still very enticing. Nevertheless, my hope is that this piece invites you to learn more about this new alternative and experiment with it, if you want. :)
Let me know whether you decide to give it a go! I’d love to hear your thoughts on it.
Catch you in the next one.
Bap
